skill.name: airflow-dag-patterns
skill.description: Build production Apache Airflow DAGs with patterns for operators, sensors, testing, scheduling, deployment, and operational safety.
skill.summary: Create reliable Apache Airflow DAGs using production workflow orchestration patterns.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high and medium patterns, but review found they are documentation examples, Markdown backticks, Airflow dependency operators, or ordinary resource links. No malicious intent, prompt injection, credential access, or hidden execution was found; the only retained concern is a placeholder HTTP polling example that users should adapt safely.

low_findings.title: Placeholder HTTP Request in Airflow Sensor Example
low_findings.description: The playbook shows an Airflow custom sensor calling https://api.example.com/health with requests.get. This is a normal DAG polling example and not executed by the skill, but copied code should add timeouts, authentication handling, and approved endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network call is directly present in a documentation code block. Its endpoint is a placeholder example domain and there is no evidence of exfiltration or malicious collection.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Airflow DAG Registration Uses globals()
low_findings.description: The globals() assignment registers dynamically generated Airflow DAG objects from a static PIPELINE_CONFIGS list. This is a common Airflow pattern and does not evaluate user-controlled code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding code shows static pipeline configuration and direct create_dag calls. No user input, eval, import loading, or command execution path is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks and Airflow Operators
low_findings.description: Static analysis treated Markdown code fences, inline code formatting, and Airflow dependency operators as shell or Ruby backtick execution. These are documentation syntax and Python Airflow examples, not executable shell commands in the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review confirms these lines are Markdown fences, inline references, directory tree text, or Airflow task dependency syntax. There is no shell interpreter invocation.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Pattern Matches
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts align with ordinary Airflow DAG wording and documentation links, not MD5, SHA1, DES, or other weak cryptographic operations. No evidence found of cryptographic code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain Airflow DAG descriptions, table text, or an Astronomer documentation URL. Targeted search found no weak cryptographic API usage.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Reconnaissance Pattern Matches
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance alerts reference DAG identifiers, task dependency assertions, a best-practice sentence, and documentation links. No evidence found of system inventory, network scanning, or host discovery behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited context is Airflow example code and safety guidance. There are no commands or APIs that enumerate hosts, ports, filesystems, users, or environment details.

content.value_statement: Airflow projects often fail because DAGs are hard to retry, test, and operate. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to produce reliable DAG patterns for real data workflows.
actual_capabilities: Designs DAG structure with schedules, dependencies, retries, and ownership fields.
Provides TaskFlow API, branching, sensor, and dynamic DAG generation patterns.
Adds testing guidance for DAG loading, dependency checks, and task logic.
Recommends operational settings for alerts, retries, timeouts, and backfill safety.
Suggests project layout patterns for DAGs, plugins, tests, and shared callbacks.
limitations: Does not deploy or run Airflow environments by itself.
Examples require adaptation for each team's providers, connections, and secrets policy.
Does not replace load testing or staging validation for production backfills.
Some snippets use placeholder endpoints and storage paths that must be changed.

use_cases.title: Create a New ETL DAG
use_cases.description: Draft a scheduled extraction, transformation, and load workflow with safe retries and clear task dependencies.

use_cases.title: Standardize Airflow Reviews
use_cases.description: Check DAGs for idempotency, timeouts, alerting, test coverage, and deployment readiness.

use_cases.title: Teach Airflow Patterns
use_cases.description: Give teams consistent examples for sensors, branching, TaskFlow tasks, and dynamic DAG factories.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a Basic DAG
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the airflow-dag-patterns skill to create a daily Airflow DAG for this workflow: [describe sources, transforms, outputs]. Include retries, ownership, and task dependencies.

prompt_templates.title: Add Sensors and Alerts
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the airflow-dag-patterns skill to update this DAG design with file or API sensors, failure callbacks, timeouts, and safe retry settings: [paste design].

prompt_templates.title: Review a Production DAG
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the airflow-dag-patterns skill to review this Airflow DAG for idempotency, catchup behavior, backfill risk, testing gaps, and operational readiness: [paste DAG summary].

prompt_templates.title: Design a DAG Factory
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the airflow-dag-patterns skill to design a dynamic DAG generation approach for these related pipelines: [list pipelines]. Include naming, schedules, tests, and limits.

output_examples.input: A team needs a daily customer ETL DAG with retries and alerting.
output: A task sequence with start, extract, transform, load, and end steps.
Recommended default arguments for retries, owner, email alerts, and retry delay.
Operational notes for catchup, max active runs, and failure callbacks.

output_examples.input: A platform team wants to review an existing DAG before release.
output: A review checklist covering idempotency, task boundaries, timeouts, and alerts.
Testing recommendations for DAG import errors and dependency structure.
Backfill and schedule risks that need owner approval.

output_examples.input: A data team needs several similar ingestion DAGs.
output: A dynamic DAG factory plan based on shared configuration.
Naming and tagging guidance for each generated workflow.
Validation steps to prevent duplicate IDs and hidden dependency issues.

best_practices: Keep DAG files lightweight and move heavy business logic into tested modules.
Use explicit retries, timeouts, alerting, and ownership for every production workflow.
Validate DAG imports, task dependencies, and backfill behavior before deployment.
anti_patterns: Do not put long-running compute work directly in the DAG definition file.
Do not enable catchup or backfills without confirming idempotency and data volume.
Do not hardcode production credentials, endpoints, or environment-specific paths in DAG code.

faq.question: What does this skill help create?
faq.answer: It helps create Apache Airflow DAG designs, examples, tests, and operational checklists for production data workflows.

faq.question: Can it run Airflow for me?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples, but it does not start services, deploy DAGs, or manage infrastructure.

faq.question: Does it support Airflow 2 patterns?
faq.answer: Yes. The playbook includes TaskFlow API examples, sensors, branching, callbacks, and dynamic DAG generation.

faq.question: Is this safe for production code?
faq.answer: It is safe as guidance, but generated DAGs must be reviewed with your connections, secrets policy, and staging data.

faq.question: Can it review existing DAGs?
faq.answer: Yes. It can check structure, retries, dependency design, testing gaps, backfill risks, and operational settings.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
